PARAGRAPH 1
To solve the problem of filtering historical dates by century efficiently, we must first understand the mathematical relationship between a calendar year and its corresponding century. The standard definition dictates that the first century spans years one through one hundred, the second century spans one hundred one through two hundred, and so forth. Consequently, the nineteenth century encompasses the years from one thousand eight hundred one to one thousand nine hundred. This means that any date string starting with the year one thousand eight hundred one belongs to the nineteenth century, while a date starting with one thousand nine hundred one belongs to the twentieth century. This distinction is crucial for accurate data analysis in historical research. The provided Python function will parse each date string to extract the year component, convert it to an integer, and then calculate the specific century using integer division logic. We will use the formula where the century equals the floor of the year minus one divided by one hundred, plus one. This approach avoids floating point errors and ensures precise categorization. The function will iterate through the provided list of date strings, applying this logic to each entry. If the calculated century matches the target integer provided by the user, the original date string is added to a new result list. This method is highly efficient because it processes the data in linear time relative to the number of dates, making it suitable for large datasets containing thousands or even millions of entries. We will also include error handling to ensure that malformed date strings do not crash the program, although the primary focus remains on speed and accuracy for valid inputs. The code will be structured to be readable and maintainable, with clear variable names that describe their purpose within the algorithm. By using list comprehension, we can further optimize the performance, as this Pythonic approach is generally faster than traditional for loops for simple filtering tasks. The solution will handle edge cases such as the very first year of a century and the very last year correctly. For instance, the year one thousand eight hundred is part of the eighteenth century, not the nineteenth. Our logic accounts for this by subtracting one from the year before performing the division. This ensures that the boundary conditions are met perfectly. The resulting list will preserve the original order of the dates, which is often important for chronological analysis. Researchers can then use this filtered list for further statistical analysis or visualization. The function is designed to be a reusable component in larger data processing pipelines.
